children.value: These analyses are conceptually (partially) adjacent to M5 in

(both ask “when does behaviour change relative to ground truth?”) but they use entirely different statistical machinery and rest on different operationalisations. Again, the aim is to provide convergent evidence (a finding that emerges both under the parametric M5 specification
under at least one of the non-parametric / semi-parametric alternatives is much more credible than any alone).

(Layer 5A), which is an offline change-point detection algorithm that is run per participant on the trial-by-trial trajectory. Detected change-points are compared to the true episode episode boundaries.

(Layer 5B). Here, for each true transition, the number of trials elapsed until the participant’s net mean falls within a tolerance window of the new target (episode mean for the respective color cue) is treated as a time-to-event outcome with right censoring.

For each participant, the per-trial signal is the signed angular distance of the participant’s net mean from a per-participant reference angle (signed difference is wrapped to get the euclidean distance instead of circular one).
segmentation (Killick, Fearnhead, & Eckley, 2012) implemented in the
Python library (Truong, Oudre, & Vayatis, 2020). PELT minimises a penalised cost function

children.value: , computed over transitions where a latency is defined (NaN for transitions never matched).
The per-transition signed latency is the unit of analysis for the parametric test. A linear mixed model is fit on the long-format per-transition table:

latency ~ condition_L + condition_Q
          + episode_index_c
          + condition_L : episode_index_c
          + condition_Q : episode_index_c
          + transition_dir_sum
          + condition_L : transition_dir_sum

The structure deliberately mirrors M5 so that interaction signs and magnitudes can be compared coefficient-by-coefficient between the two analyses (for cross-validation). Transitions where no detection occurred (NaN latency) are excluded from this model and tracked separately in the companion model below.
M_CP_miss as companion binomial model on hit rate

children.label: M_CP_miss as companion binomial model on hit rate

children.value: Per-transition hit indicators are modelled with a binomial GLMM (logit link), using the same predictor structure as M_CP. The fitting strategy mirrors M7’s:
(variational Bayes) is the preferred fit, with plain logistic regression as a fallback.
This companion model is informative because a participant who fails to detect a transition does not contribute a “long latency” - they contribute
latency. Treating the hit rate separately (rather than imputing latency for misses) can (hopefully) handle this in a pricipled manner.
Long-format table: one row per (participant, true transition)
Wide summary: hit rate, FA rate, mean abs latency per participant
Per-participant trajectory plots overlaying detected CPs and true transitions
Aggregate panels: latency distributions, hit rate by condition, latency × episode_index

children.value: . Averaging across cues produces the latent-state-induced reference (rather than a cue-specific reference), which is the relevant target for “did the participant reach the new state?”. Episodes with fewer than 3 delivered outcomes are excluded. #CHECK

children.value: If the threshold is never reached within the episode, the observation is
is 1 if the threshold was reached, 0 if right-censored.

, both reported. The two thresholds are implement to represent two layered questions. The 30° threshold asks “did the participant reach the right region?” while the 15° threshold asks “did the participant consolidate tightly?”. Consistent vs divergent effects between the two thresholds could themselves be informative.
The semiparametric Cox proportional hazards model (Cox, 1972) is fit at each threshold:

Each participant contributes 7 (8! CHECK) transitions. The Cox partial likelihood treats observations as independent, which understates SEs in the presence of within-participant correlation. We use
. This is a sandwich-type robust variance estimator analogous to those used in GEE, and is the standard alternative when frailty terms are not natively supported (which they are not in
at the time of writing).( (So this is the survival-analysis analogue of the cluster-robust OLS fallback used in the GLMMs in the framework)

children.value: is the hazard ratio per unit increase in the covariate.
(a positive coefficient means recovery is faster in higher-instruction conditions, on the unit-contrast scale).
Two Kaplan–Meier survival curve plots accompany the Cox model:

transitions, with an embedded log-rank test (Mantel, 1966) per panel. This directly addresses / allows to control for potential structural-asymmetry effects (return-to-baseline transitions might be reached faster than away-from-baseline transitions because baseline is the perceptually familiar configuration)
Long-format: one row per (participant, episode, threshold)

How M5, M_CP, and M_SURV relate

children.label: How M5, M_CP, and M_SURV relate

children.value: Overall, each addresses a different facet of the same underlying construct (latent-state inference / cross-cue generalisation):

children.html: c2c_2c2​

How long until the participant’s net mean reaches the new target?
Semi-parametric (Cox PH), defines an explicit event with censoring

Findings that converge across all three are robust to operationalisation. Dissociations are themselves diagnostic. (e.g., a participant with low M_CP latency (detects transitions quickly) but long M_SURV recovery time (slow to consolidate) has a different cognitive/behavioral profile from one with the opposite pattern, mapping onto separate model parameters in the BayesFlow generative-model fits (
